CALCIFICATION: Pathologic deposition of mineral salts in soft tissues
BRAIN: Central organ of the nervous system within the cranium
NEURON: Electrically excitable cell transmitting nerve impulses
AXON: Long projection carrying signals away from the cell body
SYNAPSE: Junction where impulses pass between two cells
CORTEX: Outer layer of gray matter in the cerebrum
MEDULLA: Brainstem region regulating vital autonomic functions
PONS: Brainstem part connecting cerebrum and cerebellum
MIDBRAIN: Superior brainstem region for eye movement and hearing
CEREBELLUM: Hindbrain structure coordinating movement and balance
THALAMUS: Relay station for sensory information to the cerebrum
HYPOTHALAMUS: Brain region controlling pituitary and homeostasis
NERVE: Bundle of peripheral axons transmitting signals
GLIA: Support cells of the nervous system
MYELIN: Insulating sheath increasing action potential speed
GYRUS: Cortical ridge separated by a sulcus
MENINGES: Three protective layers enclosing the CNS
DURA: Toughest outermost brain and cord covering
ARACHNOID: Middle weblike layer of brain and cord coverings
PIA: Innermost delicate layer adhering to the brain
CEREBRUM: Largest brain part, responsible for higher functions
CEREBRAL: Relating to the largest part of the brain
SPINAL: Pertaining to the vertebral column's nerve cord
REFLEX: Involuntary, rapid response to a stimulus
MOTOR: Nerve fibers carrying efferent signals from CNS
SENSORY: Afferent neurons conveying information to CNS
SOMA: Cell body of a neuron, containing the nucleus
NEUROGLIA: Supportive non-neuronal cells of the nervous system
TRACT: Bundle of nerve fibers in the CNS
NUCLEUS: Collection of neuronal cell bodies in the CNS
RETINA: Light-sensitive tissue at the back of the eye
OPTIC: Cranial nerve II, transmitting visual information
CAUDA: Bundle of spinal nerve roots below the conus medullaris
LIMBIC: System involved in emotion, motivation, and memory
EXCITOTOXICITY: Neuronal damage from excessive neurotransmitter stimulation
CISTERNOSTOMY: Surgical creation of an opening into a subarachnoid space
TRACTOGRAPHY: MRI technique mapping white matter pathways
AVM: Congenital tangle of abnormal vessels in the brain
OPSOCLONUS: Chaotic, rapid eye movements, often paraneoplastic
DECUSSATION: Crossing of nerve fibers from one side to the other
BRONCHUS: Large airway extending from the trachea
ALVEOLUS: Single functional unit of gas exchange in the lung
PLEURA: Serous membrane surrounding the lungs and thoracic cavity
TRACHEA: Windpipe supported by C-shaped hyaline cartilage rings
LARYNX: Voice box superior to the windpipe
PHARYNX: Throat, shared by both respiratory and digestive systems
DIAPHRAGM: Primary muscle of quiet breathing, innervated by phrenic nerve
STERNUM: Manubrium, body, and xiphoid process make up this bone
OXYGEN: Gas transported by hemoglobin for cellular metabolism
CARBON: Element found in the waste product of cellular respiration
DIOXIDE: Compound exhaled as a metabolic waste product
DIFFUSION: Passive movement of gases across the alveolar-capillary membrane
RESPIRATION: Process of gas exchange between an organism and its environment
INSPIRATION: Active process involving muscle contraction to draw air in
EXPIRATION: Passive process of air leaving the lungs during quiet breathing
TIDAL: Volume of air moved in and out during a normal breath
VITAL: Total lung capacity minus residual volume
FEV: Volume exhaled in the first second of a forced breath
FVC: Total volume exhaled during a maximally forced breath
PEAKFLOW: Maximum speed of exhalation, often measured at home
SPIROMETRY: Pulmonary function test measuring lung volumes and flow rates
CILIA: Hair-like structures clearing mucus in airways
SPUTUM: Mucus expectorated from the respiratory tract
HILUM: Entry point for vessels and airways into the lung
APEX: Uppermost part of the lung, susceptible to TB
BASE: Inferior surface of the lung, resting on the diaphragm
LOBE: Large anatomical division of the lung
SEGMENT: Bronchopulmonary unit supplied by a tertiary bronchus
ACINUS: Functional unit distal to a terminal bronchiole
PNEUMOCYTE: Cell lining the alveoli, type II produces surfactant
PLEURITIS: Inflammation causing sharp chest pain with breathing
ASTHMA: Reversible airway obstruction with bronchial hyperresponsiveness
ATELECTASIS: Collapsed lung tissue often due to airway obstruction
HEMOTHORAX: Blood accumulation within the pleural space
SILICOSIS: Lung fibrosis from inhaling crystalline silica
CONSOLIDATION: Alveolar spaces filled with fluid or exudate
PULMONARY: Relating to the lungs or respiratory system
LOBECTOMY: Surgical removal of a lung segment
CRYOBIOPSY: Frozen tissue sampling method for lung disease
VOLUTRAUMA: Lung injury from excessive ventilator tidal volumes
ESOPHAGEAL: Pertaining to the muscular tube connecting pharynx to stomach
TREPROSTINIL: Prostacyclin analogue for managing pulmonary hypertension
ENDOTHELIN: Potent vasoconstrictor peptide in vascular tone
INTERSTITIAL: Space between alveoli and capillaries in the lungs
MOUTH: Oral cavity, site of initial digestion
TONGUE: Muscular organ for taste, speech, and swallowing
ESOPHAGUS: Muscular tube transporting food to the stomach
PUSTULE: Small, circumscribed elevation of skin containing purulent exudate
WHEAL: Transient, edematous papule or plaque, often pruritic
SCALE: Flakes of stratum corneum, seen in psoriasis or seborrhea
CRUST: Dried serum, blood, or pus on the skin surface
DERMATITIS: Inflammation of the skin, often presenting with erythema and pruritus
ERYSIPELAS: Superficial cellulitis with sharply demarcated, raised borders
CANDIDIASIS: Opportunistic fungal infection causing thrush or diaper rash
KERATOSIS: Horny growth, often benign, resulting from excess keratin
ICHTHYOSIS: Genetic disorder causing dry, fish-like skin
ANHIDROSIS: Inability to sweat normally, causing heat intolerance
LASER: Device using focused light for precise tissue destruction or cutting
FLAP: Tissue moved with its own blood supply for reconstruction
PERIOSTEUM: Fibrous membrane covering the outer surface of bone
ENDOSTEUM: Membrane lining the medullary cavity of long bones
OSTEON: Haversian system, the basic structural unit of compact bone
ACETABULUM: Socket on the pelvis articulating with the femoral head
STOMACH: J-shaped organ where protein digestion begins
LIVER: Largest internal organ, detoxifies blood and produces bile
GALLBLADDER: Pear-shaped organ storing and concentrating bile
PANCREAS: Organ producing digestive enzymes and hormones like insulin
SPLEEN: Lymphoid organ filtering blood and removing old red cells
CHYME: Acidic fluid entering the duodenum from the gaster
AMYLASE: Enzyme initiating carbohydrate breakdown in saliva
LIPASE: Enzyme essential for fat digestion in the small intestine
TRYPSIN: Pancreatic enzyme cleaving proteins into smaller peptides
PEPSIN: Chief cells secrete this protein-digesting enzyme precursor
GASTRIN: Hormone stimulating acid secretion by parietal cells
SECRETIN: Duodenal hormone stimulating bicarbonate release from the pancreas
MESENTERY: Fold of peritoneum attaching the intestine to posterior abdominal wall
PERITONEUM: Serous membrane lining the abdominal cavity
OMENTUM: Fatty apron-like fold of peritoneum covering abdominal organs
VILLUS: Finger-like projection increasing surface area in the small intestine
CRYPT: Invagination between intestinal projections containing stem cells
ENTERIC: Pertaining to the small intestine or its nervous system
MOTILITY: Movement of food through the alimentary canal
ABSORPTION: Process of nutrients entering the bloodstream from the lumen
PYLORUS: Distal part of the gaster controlling emptying into duodenum
FUNDUS: Dome-shaped upper part of the gaster
GASTRITIS: Inflammation of the lining of the gaster
